1.title_ru: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
1.topic_ru: Electron configuration, isotopes, ionization energies, periodic trends
algorithm_ru: Read the problem carefully — determine what it asks about: electron configuration, isotopes, or periodic trends.

For electron configuration: write subshells in order following the aufbau principle (1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → ...). Number of shells = period number. Sum of valence electrons (s + p in the outermost shell) = group number (s and p blocks).
For isotopes: remember that Z = number of protons = number of electrons (neutral atom), A = Z + N (neutrons).
For trends: across a period (→) ionization energy and electronegativity increase, atomic radius decreases. Down a group (↓) the opposite.
Watch for anomalies: e.g. IE(Al) < IE(Mg) due to the full 3s² subshell of magnesium.
key_facts_ru: Electron configuration is built following the aufbau principle: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p...
Hund's rule: electrons occupy separate orbitals of the same subshell before pairing up.
Pauli exclusion principle: two electrons in the same orbital must have opposite spins.
Isotopes are atoms of the same element that differ in the number of neutrons.
Ionization energy generally increases across a period and decreases down a group, but there are exceptions (full and half-full subshells are more stable).
common_traps_ru: Do not confuse mass number (A = p + n) with atomic number (Z = p).
Remember the ionization energy anomalies (e.g. B < Be, O < N).
For d-block elements, ion configuration differs from the atom — s electrons are removed first (e.g. Fe²⁺: [Ar]3d⁶, not [Ar]3d⁴4s²).
1.variability_ru: The element, question type (configuration, isotopes, trend comparison) varies. The principles are universal.

2.topic_ru: Bond types (ionic, covalent, metallic, coordinate), hybridization, hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces
algorithm_ru: Determine the bond type from the electronegativity difference: ΔEN > 1.7 → ionic; 0.4 < ΔEN < 1.7 → polar covalent; ΔEN < 0.4 → nonpolar covalent; metal–metal → metallic.
Coordinate (dative) bond: one atom supplies both electrons of the bonding pair (e.g. NH₄⁺, H₃O⁺, transition metal complexes).
Intermolecular interactions: hydrogen bonds (N–H···X, O–H···X, F–H···X) > dipole–dipole > dispersion (London) forces.
When comparing boiling points: stronger interactions → higher T_b. Hydrogen bonds significantly raise the boiling point.
In descriptive questions: always refer to specific interactions and provide justification (electronegativity, lone pairs, atomic size).
key_facts_ru: Ionic bond: between a metal and a nonmetal (large ΔEN), forms an ionic crystal.
Covalent bond: sharing of electron pairs between nonmetals.
Metallic bond: a 'sea' of delocalized valence electrons among metal cations.
The O–H···O hydrogen bond is the strongest type of hydrogen bond in inorganic chemistry.
A coordinate bond, once formed, is indistinguishable from a regular covalent bond.
common_traps_ru: Do not confuse intermolecular interactions (weak) with chemical bonds (strong).
HF has a higher T_b than HCl, but lower than H₂O — despite F being more electronegative, H₂O forms more hydrogen bonds (2 lone pairs, 2 H atoms).
In questions about coordinate bonds, remember NH₄⁺ and H₃O⁺ — these are classic examples.
2.variability_ru: The set of substances and bond type to match varies. Questions about hybridization (sp, sp², sp³), molecular shape, and polarity may appear.

3.topic_ru: Molar mass, mole, molar volume, percent composition, calculations from chemical equations
algorithm_ru: Write down the given and required quantities. Determine which conversion is needed.
Molar mass: sum the atomic masses of all atoms in the formula. Remember subscripts and parentheses.
Calculations from equations: (1) write the balanced equation, (2) calculate n = m/M or n = V/V_m, (3) convert moles using stoichiometric coefficients, (4) calculate the required quantity.
Molar volume of a gas at STP: V_m = 22.4 dm³/mol.
Mass fraction of an element: ω = (n × M_element) / M_compound × 100%.
key_facts_ru: 1 mole = 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number).
n = m/M (moles = mass/molar mass); n = V/V_m (for gases at STP).
Law of conservation of mass: mass of reactants = mass of products.
Avogadro's law: at the same T and p, equal volumes of gases contain the same number of molecules.
Law of definite proportions (Proust): a chemical compound has a constant quantitative composition.
common_traps_ru: Don't forget parentheses in formulas — e.g. (NH₄)₂SO₄ has 2 N atoms and 8 H atoms.
V_m = 22.4 dm³/mol applies only at STP (0 °C, 1013 hPa).
Check that the equation is balanced before starting calculations.
3.variability_ru: The substance and calculation type (molar mass, gas volume, percent composition, equation-based calculation) varies. The conversion principles are the same.

4.topic_ru: Acid-base theories, pH, salt hydrolysis, buffer solutions
algorithm_ru: Determine acid/base strength: strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HClO₄) dissociate completely; weak acids (CH₃COOH, H₂CO₃, HF) — partially.
pH = −log[H⁺]. For strong acids: [H⁺] = acid concentration. For strong bases: [OH⁻] = base concentration, pOH = −log[OH⁻], pH = 14 − pOH.
Strength of oxyacids: increases with the number of oxygen atoms not bonded to hydrogen (more O → stronger acid).
Salt hydrolysis: salt of weak acid + strong base → pH > 7 (basic); salt of strong acid + weak base → pH < 7 (acidic); both strong → pH ≈ 7.
When comparing acid strength, consider the oxidation state of the central atom and the stability of the anion.
key_facts_ru: pH scale: 0–14; pH < 7 → acidic, pH = 7 → neutral, pH > 7 → basic.
Binary acids (HF, HCl, HBr, HI): strength increases down the group (HF < HCl < HBr < HI).
Oxyacids of the same element: strength increases with oxidation state (HClO < HClO₂ < HClO₃ < HClO₄).
A buffer solution: maintains approximately constant pH upon addition of small amounts of acid or base.
common_traps_ru: HF is a weak acid, despite fluorine being the strongest oxidizer — don't confuse electronegativity with acid strength.
Don't confuse 'concentrated' acid with 'strong' — these are different concepts (concentration vs. degree of dissociation).
When calculating the pH of a salt solution, you must account for hydrolysis — knowing the salt concentration alone is not enough.
4.variability_ru: The acid/base/salt and question type (strength comparison, pH calculation, predicting solution reaction) varies. The classification and calculation principles are universal.

5.topic_ru: Oxidation states, electron balance, oxidizing and reducing agents, balancing redox equations
algorithm_ru: Determine the oxidation states of all atoms in reactants and products.
Identify which atom changes its oxidation state — determine who is the oxidizing agent (decreases o.s., gains e⁻) and who is the reducing agent (increases o.s., loses e⁻).
Write the half-reactions of oxidation and reduction with the number of electrons transferred.
Balance the electrons: find the LCM of electrons lost and gained.
Balance the equation: coefficients from the electron balance → fill in the remaining atoms (H, O) using H₂O and H⁺ (or OH⁻ in basic media).
key_facts_ru: Oxidation = loss of electrons = increase in oxidation state.
Reduction = gain of electrons = decrease in oxidation state.
Typical oxidizing agents: KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇, HNO₃(conc.), H₂SO₄(conc.), Cl₂, O₂.
Typical reducing agents: active metals (Zn, Fe, Al), H₂S, SO₂, C, CO.
In disproportionation reactions, the same element is simultaneously oxidized and reduced.
common_traps_ru: Don't confuse the oxidizing agent (the substance that is reduced) with oxidation (the process of losing electrons).
When balancing reactions with HNO₃: some nitrogen acts as the oxidizer (→ NO, NO₂) and some forms the salt — account for both roles.
The oxidation state of H is almost always +1, O almost always −2 (exceptions: NaH → H⁻¹, H₂O₂ → O⁻¹, OF₂ → O⁺²).
5.variability_ru: The redox reaction, the atom in question, and the format (oxidation states, balancing, identifying the oxidizer/reducer) varies. The electron balance method is universal.

6.topic_ru: Functional groups, nomenclature, organic reactions (substitution, addition, elimination, esterification), identification
algorithm_ru: Identify the functional group: −OH (alcohol/phenol), −CHO (aldehyde), −CO− (ketone), −COOH (carboxylic acid), −NH₂ (amine), −COO− (ester).
Characteristic reactions: aldehydes → Tollens' test (silver mirror) and Fehling's test; alkenes → decolorization of bromine water; alcohols → test with Na (H₂ evolution).
Esterification: acid + alcohol ⇌ ester + H₂O (catalyst: conc. H₂SO₄, reversible reaction).
Polymers: addition (polyaddition) reaction — e.g. polyethylene from ethylene; condensation (polycondensation) reaction — e.g. PET, nylon.
IUPAC nomenclature: find the longest chain, number from the end closest to the substituent, name substituents with prefixes (methyl-, ethyl-...).
key_facts_ru: Homologous series: alkanes (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂), alkenes (CₙH₂ₙ), alkynes (CₙH₂ₙ₋₂).
Tollens' test detects aldehydes (and glucose — aldose sugars) — metallic silver is deposited.
Lucas test: distinguishes primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols by reaction rate with HCl/ZnCl₂.
Primary alcohols → oxidation → aldehyde → further oxidation → carboxylic acid.
Isomerism: structural (chain, positional, functional group) and geometric (cis/trans).
common_traps_ru: Acetone (propanone) is a ketone — it does NOT give a positive Tollens' test (unlike aldehydes).
Phenol (C₆H₅OH) has acidic properties, even though it contains an −OH group like alcohols.
Glucose has an open-chain form with a −CHO group, so it gives a positive Tollens' test, even though the cyclic form predominates in solution.
6.variability_ru: The type of organic compound, reaction, and identification method varies. The key is recognizing functional groups and knowing the characteristic reactions of each class.

7.topic_ru: Reaction rate, factors affecting rate, chemical equilibrium, Le Chatelier's principle, equilibrium constant
algorithm_ru: Factors affecting reaction rate: temperature, concentration (pressure for gases), contact surface area, catalyst, nature of reactants.
Van't Hoff's rule: a 10 °C temperature increase raises the rate 2–4 times.
Le Chatelier's principle: a system at equilibrium responds to a change in conditions so as to partially counteract that change.
Equilibrium shift: ↑pressure → toward fewer moles of gas; ↑temperature → toward the endothermic reaction; ↑reactant concentration → toward products.
A catalyst does NOT shift the equilibrium — it accelerates both the forward and reverse reactions and shortens the time to reach equilibrium.
key_facts_ru: K_c = [products]ⁿ / [reactants]ᵐ — the equilibrium constant (depends only on temperature).
K_c > 1 → equilibrium favors products; K_c < 1 → equilibrium favors reactants.
A catalyst lowers the activation energy but does not change ΔH or K_c.
Exothermic reaction (ΔH < 0): raising temperature shifts the equilibrium to the left (toward reactants).
Pressure change affects equilibrium only when the number of gaseous moles of reactants ≠ number of gaseous moles of products.
common_traps_ru: A catalyst does NOT shift the equilibrium — this is the most common exam mistake.
Adding a solid (e.g. carbon in C + CO₂ ⇌ 2CO) does not shift the equilibrium, because the concentration of a solid is constant.
When analyzing the effect of pressure: count gaseous moles on both sides of the equation (ignore solids and liquids).
7.variability_ru: The equilibrium reaction and factor type (T, p, concentration, catalyst) varies. Le Chatelier's principle is always the same.

8.topic_ru: Galvanic cells, electrolysis, standard electrode potentials, corrosion, metal protection
algorithm_ru: Galvanic cell: the metal with the lower E° is the anode (oxidation), the metal with the higher E° is the cathode (reduction). EMF = E°(cathode) − E°(anode).
Electrolysis: the reverse of a galvanic cell — requires electrical energy input. At the cathode: reduction (metal deposition or H₂ evolution). At the anode: oxidation.
Electrolysis of aqueous solutions: at the cathode, either the metal or H₂ may be produced (depends on the metal's E°); at the anode: O₂, Cl₂, or oxidation of the anode itself.
Corrosion: a spontaneous electrochemical process. Protection: coatings, sacrificial anode (galvanizing), cathodic protection.
Faraday's law: m = (M × I × t) / (n × F), where F = 96,500 C/mol.
key_facts_ru: Electrochemical series (selected): Li < K < Ca < Na < Mg < Al < Zn < Fe < Ni < Sn < Pb < H₂ < Cu < Ag < Au.
EMF (electromotive force) = E°(cathode) − E°(anode); EMF > 0 → the cell operates spontaneously.
Electrolysis of NaCl(aq): cathode → H₂, anode → Cl₂ (not O₂, due to anodic overpotential).
Galvanizing = sacrificial protection: Zn (E° = −0.76 V) corrodes instead of Fe (E° = −0.44 V).
1 Faraday (F) = 96,500 C/mol = the charge of 1 mole of electrons.
common_traps_ru: Don't confuse anode and cathode: in a galvanic cell the anode is negative (−), in an electrolytic cell it's positive (+), but always: oxidation occurs at the anode.
In aqueous electrolysis: metals with E° > E°(H₂O/H₂) ≈ −0.83 V are deposited at the cathode; more active metals → H₂ is evolved instead.
EMF must be positive for the cell to work spontaneously. If you calculate a negative EMF, you've swapped the anode and cathode.
8.variability_ru: The metal pair in the cell, electrolyte in electrolysis, and corrosion protection method varies. The principles of cell and electrolysis operation remain constant.
